coredata.dc:title: Enhanced humidity-sensing performance with metallurgy-derived ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdo, Dina M.
Rasly, Mahmoud
Morsy, Mohamed
El-Shazly, Ayat N.
coredata.dc:description: A cost effective relative humidity sensor based on ZnO/graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs) was developed by the nanoarchitecturally-metallurgy techniques. Nanospherical-like structure of ZnO powders was prepared from Zn-rich Egyptian ore using the co-precipitation technique. Mono-dispersed graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were optimized by pyrolysis of citric acid. After forming the nanocomposite using a metallurgical pathway, the sensors were fabricated via a simple spin-coating technique. The sensors were tested across a broad humidity range (11 % to 97 % RH) at room temperature, with their sensing behavior evaluated at different frequencies to determine the optimal operating conditions. The humidity sensing characteristics of prepared materials, including sensitivity, stability, hysteresis, response/recovery times, and repeatability, were measured. The sensitivity of fabricated ZnO/GQDs sensors is 0.18 MΩ/RH, with faster response and recovery time than bare ZnO, enabling high robustness and sensing performance. These findings demonstrate an enhanced-sensing performance with ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite from cost-effective resources, providing a promising avenue for the development of on-chip humidity sensors with fast response, and repeatability over a wide range of humidity levels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
Egyptian ore
GQDs
Nanocomposite
Humidity sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite.
XRD patterns of the ZnO (a) and ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite (b).
High-resolution TEM image and size distribution histogram of GQDs (a,b) and High-resolution TEM image of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs (c, d) respectively.
XPS spectra of ZnO and ZnO/GQD nanocomposite. The survey (a), High-resolution Zn 2p spectra, (b) the deconvoluted O1s spectra (c), and, C1s high-resolution spectra (d).
The humidity sensing behavior of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs at different testing frequencies (a,b) and optimum frequency of 100 Hz (c).
The repeatability for six cycles at humidity levels 11 % and 43 % (a) ZnO and (b) ZnO/GQDs.
Response and recovery times of (a) ZnO and (b) ZnO/GQDs.
The short-term stability of (a) ZnO and (b) ZnO/GQDs.
The relation between the real part and the imaginary part of impedance at different humidity levels, low humidity (a) and high humidity (b).
Humidity sensing mechanism for the fabricated sensing materials.

head.ce:title: Enhanced humidity-sensing performance with metallurgy-derived ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Metallurgical Research and Development Institute, P.O. Box: 87, Helwan, 11421 Cairo, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central Metallurgical Research and Development Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Building Physics and Environment Institute, Housing and Building National Research Center (HBRC), Dokki, Giza, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center (NTRC), The British University in Egypt (BUE), El Sherouk City, Suez Desert Road, Cairo 11837, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Center (NTRC), The British University in Egypt (BUE), El Sherouk City, Suez Desert Road, Cairo 11837, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A cost effective relative humidity sensor based on ZnO/graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs) was developed by the nanoarchitecturally-metallurgy techniques. Nanospherical-like structure of ZnO powders was prepared from Zn-rich Egyptian ore using the co-precipitation technique. Mono-dispersed graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were optimized by pyrolysis of citric acid. After forming the nanocomposite using a metallurgical pathway, the sensors were fabricated via a simple spin-coating technique. The sensors were tested across a broad humidity range (11 % to 97 % RH) at room temperature, with their sensing behavior evaluated at different frequencies to determine the optimal operating conditions. The humidity sensing characteristics of prepared materials, including sensitivity, stability, hysteresis, response/recovery times, and repeatability, were measured. The sensitivity of fabricated ZnO/GQDs sensors is 0.18 MΩ/RH, with faster response and recovery time than bare ZnO, enabling high robustness and sensing performance. These findings demonstrate an enhanced-sensing performance with ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite from cost-effective resources, providing a promising avenue for the development of on-chip humidity sensors with fast response, and repeatability over a wide range of humidity levels.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Humidity sensors are an integral part of our daily lives. They are found in a variety of applications ranging from environmental monitoring, pharmaceuticals, and the health sector, semicosnductor industry, food processing, aerospace industry, building and construction industries, fuel industry, and geographic information systems [1]. Hence, the development of reliable humidity sensors is of utmost importance, and a lot of research is being carried out in this field. The humidity-sensitive materials, which serve as the core of the humidity sensors, can show significant adsorption to the water molecules and, in turn, influence the physical property such as electrical resistivity [2]. Selected materials, such as metal oxides, have been explored for humidity sensing applications. In particular, ZnO, TiO2, CuO, and Al2O3 are widely used for sensor development due to their simple fabrication, high sensing response, good operational stability, low cost, and good portability [3–6]. Among them, zinc oxide (ZnO) is a superior material for humidity sensors because of its high electron mobility, non-toxicity, cost efficiency, and thermal stability [7,8]. However, the demerits of pure ZnO as a humidity-sensitive material are the poor linearity of resistance response, ideally the change in impedance, and long recovery time due to hysteresis associated with the absorption of water molecules [9]. These parameters hinder its practical applications in harsh environments.

In recent years, extensive efforts have been made to explore two-dimensional (2D) carbon nanomaterials such as graphene for humidity sensing applications [10]. These 2D materials have excellent electron transport properties and a high surface-to-volume ratio [11]. However, the weak interaction between carbon nanomaterials and water molecules is a limiting factor in the production of high-quality humidity sensors with high performance and fast response. Inherently, the recombination process of electron-hole pair is one of the main barriers to increase the humidity-sensing performance of semiconductors. For that, to improve the humidity-sensing properties of pure graphene, heterostructural compounds of graphene and metal oxide semiconductors have been explored [12], where, graphene/metal oxide semiconductors are very crucial to boost the unique electronic, structural, and microstructural characteristics of graphene with the needed sensing properties of the metal oxide nanostructures [13–15]. Specifically, the development of nanoarchitecturally graphene oxide (GO)/Zinc oxide nanocomposite (ZnO/GO nanocomposite) has been carried out using solvothermal [16], hydrothermal [17,18], sol-gel [19], and microwave-assisted techniques [20]. However, most of these techniques explored sophisticated procedures and required high-grade raw materials for processing. With those wet-chemical routes also, the affinity of ZnO to fill the pores and form dense structures with graphene owing to van Der Waals interaction is high. This results in a dramatic decrease in surface area and pore volume. Three-dimensional graphene foam (3DGF) possesses a relatively high specific surface area and large pore volume, enabling excellent circumstances for the fabrication of humidity sensors [21]. ZnO-decorated 3D GF-based humidity sensors have been developed and they showed a relatively wide humidity sensing range, from 0 % to far beyond 85.9 % [15]. However, the cost of production of thin film 3D GF was high and technologically advanced; it needs ultra-high grade chemicals, and a hydrothermal process to mix graphene with ZnO is not suitable for mass production.

Very recently, extensive efforts have been made in humidity sensor-based metal oxide quantum dots (MOQDs), e.g., particles are a few nanometers in size. GQDs have an advantage in terms of effective interfacing over graphene (3D GF) because of a sufficient number of active sites to uniformly cover the metal oxide nanostructured [22], resulting in improved performance and promising solutions as a humidity sensing medium. These materials possess customized properties and promising solutions, such as humidity sensors [23]. Also, growth-engineered lateral graphene quantum dots (LGQDs), e.g., the lateral sizes are in order of a few nanometers, evinces unique properties and functionalities over micrometer-sized graphene oxides (GOs) [24]. However, several sophisticated chemical steps and processes are involved to fabricate these sensors. So, searching for alternative cost-effective techniques and superior materials is indispensable.

We previously extracted ZnO powders from natural Egyptian ore and successfully converted it to nanopowders using the mass-production techniques as a co-precipitation technique [25]. In the meantime, mono-dispersed GQDs have the privilege of (LGQDs) in the aspect of anti-agglomeration [26]. In the present study, ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite has been synthesized to be used as efficient humidity sensor. ZnO NPs were synthesized from Egyptian natural ore using an eco-friendly co-precipitation method, while graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by performing a pyrolysis process for the citric acid. The ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite was obtained by grinding 10 % (wt) GQDs with 90 % (wt) ZnO NPs manually through an agate mortar. The obtained nanocomposite has been characterized using XRD, TEM, and XPS to confirm its structure. The humidity-sensing characteristics of the ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite were characterized by a wide range of relative humidity/air levels from 11 % up to 97 %. The optimum testing frequency was determined and found to be 100 Hz. The generated data confirmed that the response and recovery time were improved due to the addition of GQDs.

Firstly, zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) were prepared from Egyptian natural ore called “Calamines” via a simple eco-friendly procedure that was reported in detail through previous work [25,27]. Zinc Egyptian ore was leached with 4 M NaOH at optimum conditions of temperature ∼ 80 °C and magnetic stirring ∼400 rpm for 3 h, followed by the synthesis of ZnO through the co-precipitation method. The leachate was precipitated using 1 M KOH solution, and a neutral pH = 6 was adjusted using HCL under continuous stirring for 2 h. The precipitate was centrifuged, washed, and kept in a dryer overnight at 60 °C. Afterward, the precursor was calcinated at 600 °C for 2 h.

Secondly, we have prepared graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through the pyrolysis of citric acid. We increased the temperature of citric acid up to 200 °C using a heating mantle for a liquefication process in a time average of ∼5 min. As a result, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow. After 30 min, the color turned to dark orange, ensuring the quasi-formation of GQDs. The solution was then gently dropped into a 100-mL solution of 0.25 M NaOH under vigorous stirring. By reaching ∼pH 5, the aqueous solution of GQDs was evaporated to the final form of the GQDs. ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite was achieved by grinding 10 % by mass GQDs with 90 % ZnO NPs through agate mortar, similar to the schematic diagram in Fig. 1 . The synthesized ZnO NPs, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite morphologies were investigated using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) of JOEL JEM-2100 at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The crystal structure of the synthesized samples was confirmed by using XRD of the PANalytical X'Pert PRO diffractometer. XPS analysis was performed using Thermo-Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi. Finally, the synthesized materials were tested as a humidity sensor in a relatively wide range of humidity/air levels, from 11 % up to 97 %. The required level of relative humidity was generated using a saturated salt solution contained in a closed vessel.

The studied humidity sensor has been fabricated through the spin coating technique. The sensing material was deposited over a conductive FTO glass substrate. The procedures for sensor fabrication include substrate cleaning, materials deposition, and drying. The FTO substrate was cleaned in an ultrasonic bath using soap solution, acetone, and deionized water and dried with nitrogen. The sensing material was mixed with a small amount of deionized water to form a paste. The FTO substrate was fixed over the rotating desk of the spin coating machine, and then a proper quantity of sensing materials was spread at a speed of 1000 rpm for 60 s to form a thick layer. The fabricated substrate was dried at 60 °C overnight. The fabricated sensor was conditioned for two days under different humidity conditions to improve the stability and enhance the signal-to-noise ratio [28–31]. All the humidity-sensing characteristics were collected at room temperature.

The crystal structure of the fabricated materials was characterized by XRD analysis. Fig. 2 shows the XRD pattern of the polycrystalline ZnO (a) and ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite (b). Both samples display a series of diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 31.71, 34.40, 36.19, 47.45, 56.51, 62.78, 65.77, 67.86, and 68.96° are assigned to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201) planes of wurtzite phase of ZnO (ICDD 04–007–1614) [32–35]. However, the relative intensities of those diffracted planes were decreased upon GQDs reinforcements, with no specific diffraction features for GQDs. This is because of its low content in the nanocomposites.

TEM analysis and diameter distribution of GQDs are shown in Fig. 3 . It could be found that the GQDs sizes were relatively uniform with sizes of 2–9 nm (Fig. 3(a)) and the average size was about 3.7 nm (see Fig. 3(b)); which is quietly similar to a size of synthesized GQDs using different techniques reported in the literature [36–39], While for synthesized ZnO, TEM analysis confirmed that it has spherical morphologies as demonstrated in Fig. 3(c) with an average particle size of 30–45 nm, On the other hand, for ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite, GQDs were relatively dispersed without agglomeration as shown in Fig. 3(d), where it confirmed that GQDs spread uniformly on the ZnO surface from the inset of Fig. 3(d).

As the XRD spectra do not reveal much information about the presence of the GQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 4 ) was performed to investigate the composition of the synthesized materials and the coexistence of GQDs. Fig. 4(a) shows the overall XPS survey of both ZnO and ZnO/GQDs, indicating the presence of zinc (Zn), and oxygen (O), besides the presence of carbon (C) in the ZnO/GQDs sample. Fig. 4(b) displays the high-resolution XPS scans of Zn 2p, revealing two peaks at 1045.48 eV and 1022.46 eV that can be ascribed to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively, which are characteristic of Zn2+ and confirm the formation of ZnO bond [11,40]. This peak separation of 23.02 eV indicates that the Zn forms a divalent oxidation state in wurtzite ZnO. Further, the slight shifting of Zn 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks to 1045.49 eV and 1022.47 eV, respectively, was obtained for the ZnO/GQDs sample. Additionally, Fig. 4(c) depicts the O 1 s core-level XPS spectra of both ZnO and ZnO/GQDs. It displays two peaks at 531.07, 532.18 eV (ZnO), and 531.12, 532.4 eV (ZnO/GQDs). The peaks at 531.07 (ZnO) and 531.12 (ZnO/GQDs) are attributed to lattice oxygen (O2−) in ZnO [12,39] whereas the peaks at 532.18 eV (ZnO) and 532.4 eV (ZnO/GQDs) are the characteristics of adsorbed water molecules on the surface of the material. The shift in the Zn 2p and O 1 s spectra to higher binding energies is due to the interfacial perturbation of the Zn–O/GQDs and the formation of the nanocomposite [41]. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the three carbon peaks located at different binding energies ∼285.79, 287.71, and 289.85 eV are assigned to CC, CO, and CO, respectively, and they were mainly agitated by GQDs [42]. Notably, it has been observed that the fitted CO peak was reduced in the ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite sample. This means that some of the oxygen-related functional groups have been transformed to the in-plane epoxy groups. Such transformation accelerated the formation of C-O-Zn bonds in the ZnO/GQDs, also observed in the O1s peak fitting in the peak at 532.4 eV [43].

The humidity-sensing behavior has been established through multiple experiments conducted on the studied structures, as mentioned in Fig. 5 . The carried-out experiments, including sensitivity, repeatability, response, and recovery time, have been conducted at room temperature. The optimum testing frequency was chosen based on the maximum impedance variations. Fig. 5(a, b) displays the change in impedance as a function of relative humidity (RH) levels at different testing frequencies for both ZnO and ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite. It was found that the impedance decreases as the RH increases from 11%RH up to 97 %RH for all applied frequencies. The applied test frequency has an obvious effect on the variation in the impedance value, which is related to the polarization of adsorbed water molecules. Besides, we observed a decrease in the impedance variation as the testing frequency increased, where the maximum impedance variation was achieved at 100 Hz, and then was considered as the optimum testing frequency. This remark was proved many times in previously published articles [15,30,31]. The optimum testing frequency demonstrates the ability of water molecules to follow the applied AC wave alternation, while with increasing the applied testing frequency, the adsorbed water molecules were not able to follow the applied signal alternation. Based on measured and reported values, the optimum testing frequency was decided to be 100 Hz for both prepared samples. A close vision focusing on the humidity-sensing behavior of both samples can reveal more details regarding the response and sensitivity. The response of bare ZnO NPs seems linear to some extent, as shown in Fig. 5(c). The relation between impedance variation and relative humidity of ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite is divided into two regions. As displayed in Fig. 5(c), in the first region up to 43 %, the sensitivity is less than that of the second region (43 %–97 %). The sensitivity “S” is estimated as the impedance variation over the entire range of humidity (11 %–97 %) and can be expressed using the following equation:
(1)
S =
Z 11
−
Z H
RH H
−
RH 11
Where Z11 and ZH represent the impedance values at 11 % RH and 97 % RH, respectively. (See Fig. 6.)
Based on the above relation, we calculated the sensitivity values for ZnO and ZnO/GQDs and found to be 2.14 MΩ/RH and 0.18 MΩ/RH, respectively.

Repetability is an important and critical factor that reflects the ability of a humidity sensor to generate consistent output signals when subjected to different RH levels across several measurement cycles. The repeatability of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs has been measured for six successful cycles between 11 and 43 %. To evaluate the repetability, both of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs humidity sensors were exposed to six successive humidity cycles varying between 11 % RH and 43 % RH at room temperature. The impedance variation was measured every 3 s at 100 HZ using an LCR bridge connected with a PC for real-time data acquisition. The investigated sensors provide good repeatability, especially ZnO/GQDs sensor. The improved repeatability of the ZnO/GQDs sensor could be due to many factors represented in the abundaned of functional groups on the surface of the improves the water molecules interaction with the surface of the sensor. Moreover, GQDs enhance the charge carrier mobility, thereby reducing baseline drift between cycles.

Response time is the time taken by the sensor to reach to 90 % of its value when subjected to a step increase in RH level. The response time measures how quickly the sensor adsorb and interact with the water molecules. While recovery time is defined as the time exerted by the sensor to return back to 10 % of its baseline value when subjected to stepdown decrease in RH level. The recovery time is an indicator of how fast the humidity level desorbs water molecules and restor its baseline value. The response and recovery times are shown in Fig. 7 . It is obvious that the ZnO/GQDs sample responds faster than the ZnO sample. The same remark was also elucidated for the recovery time. The calculated response and recovery time for ZnO were 70 Sec and 220 Sec, while that for ZnO/GQDs were 30 s and 160 s. the improvement in response time of ZnO/GQDs is due to the presence of functional group on the surface of GQDs that enabling faster and more reversible water molecule interactions.

For a comprehensive evaluation of a humidity sensor, stability is a critical performance metric that requires careful consideration. The stability of a humidity sensor can be classified into two distinct regimes: short-term stability and long-term stability. Short-term stability reflects the drift of the sensor's output signal over brief durations (ranging from hours to days), while long-term stability assesses the material's degradation over extended periods (weeks to months), often evaluated through shelf-life testing. The short-term stability of the humidity sensor has been measured for ZnO and ZnO/GQDs sensors as indicated in Fig. 8 . The short-term stability of both sensors revealed a realiable and stable output signal with low drift.

Hysteresis is one of the most important parameters that deserves remarkable attention, and is defined as the phenomenon where the sensor's output reading differs depending on whether the humidity is increasing or decreasing. Hysteresis can be imagined as an indicator for the speed and manner in which a humidity sensor absorbs and releases water molecules. Hysteresis (H) is calculated using the following equation:
H =
Z des
−
Z ads
S
Where S is the sensitivity, Zdes and Zdes are the impedance values during adsorption and desorption of water molecules, respectively. The hysteresis of fabricated humidity sensors is shown in Fig. 9 . The ZnO sensor exhibited a hysteresis value of 6.7 % at 11 % RH, whereas the ZnO/GQDs composite showed a significantly lower hysteresis of 2.5 % at the same humidity level. This improvement indicates that the incorporation of GQDs into ZnO enhances the reversibility of water molecule adsorption/desorption, due to improved surface interactions and faster kinetics.

Hysteresis is a key factor that determine the ability of the sensor to reversibly respond to humidity changes. A lower hysteresis value suggests less energy dissipation during the adsorption-desorption cycle, leading to more reliable and stable sensor performance.

The response and recovery times of studied sensors is shown in Fig. 9 (a,b) for ZnO and ZnO/GQDs respectively. The response and recovery times of ZnO was estimated and found to be 7 s and 220 s, respectively, while the response and recovery times for ZnO/GQDs was found to be 30 s and 160 s, respectively. It is obvious that both of response and recovery times were improved significantly due to the addition of GQDs to ZnO.

In this section, we clarify the sensing mechanism in terms of the fundamental physics beyond the complex impedance (CIS) measurements. The humidity sensing mechanism relies on the interaction between adsorbed water molecules and active sites on the surface of the sensing material. Shown in Fig. 10 is the relationship between the real and imaginary parts of complex impedance along the x-axis and y-axis, respectively, at (a) the low and (b) high humidity levels. It is apparent that at low humidity levels, the CIS curves at low humidity up to 43 % demonstrate a semi-circle (Fig. 10a), while at high levels of humidity (Fig. 10b), a straight line is developed. The semi-circle of the CIS curve (Fig. 10a) indicates that the impedance of the sensor is governed by a combination of resistive and capacitive components. The water molecules that were adsorbed on top of the sensory-activated surface area can be seen as sequential layers. The first layer is bonded chemically via a double hydrogen bond, while the subsequent layers are physically adsorbed.

When the sensor is subjected to humid air, the first water molecules are bonded to the surface of the sensor via a double hydrogen bond. Once this layer is formed, it remains on the surface of the sensor and cannot be removed under ambient conditions. Herein, the conduction mechanism is ionic, and the concentration of charge carriers is low due to the little amount of adsorbed water molecules. Remarkably, the lowly oxidized GQDs fabricated in this study can be a promising medium to host metal oxides owing to their well-controlled energy states from subdomains that only appear within GQDs under controlled oxidation, compared with defect-rich GQDs synthesized using Hummers' method [44–46]. Typically, as displayed in Fig. 11 , the adsorbed water molecules are bonded to the surface of the sensor through van der Waals forces, which are weaker than other chemical interactions [47–49]. Under these circumstances, the hydroxyl groups are generated and interacted with subsequent adsorbed layers to form hydronium ions. These hydroniums, in turn, dissociate the adsorbed water layers to form additional hydroniums. This reaction is described as a chain reaction and is known as the Grotthuss reaction. This mechanism involves the rapid hopping of protons between water molecules, pointedly boosting the ionic conductivity of the sensor. The overall mechanism can be described using the following Eqs. [50–52]:
(1)
2H 2
O →
H 3
O +
+
OH −
(2)
H 3
O +
→
H +
+
H 2
O
(3)
H 3
O +
+
H 2
O →
H 3
O +
+
H 2
O

Zinc oxide nanoparticles have been recovered from the Egyptian ore using a simple co-precipitation route, while graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared through pyrolysis of citric acid. The ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite was synthesized by manually grinding the constant constituents in a morter-keeping a weight ratio of 10 % of GQDs. The average particle size of ZnO and GQDs, as measured from HRTEM, was 37 nm and 3.7 nm, respectively. The composition of the ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite was accurately verified through the XPS survey. In addition to the presence of Zinc and Oxygen, the high-resolution scan of the C 1 s region shows three carbon peaks located at different binding energies, which are 285.79, 287.71, and 289.85 eV, attributed to CC, CO, and CO, respectively, were mainly contributed by GQDs. The humidity sensing behavior of both ZnO and ZnO/GQDs was evaluated in a wide range of humidity from 11 % up to 97 % under different testing frequencies. The optimum testing frequency was chosen as 100 Hz based on the measurements that were performed. The calculated values of sensitivity for ZnO and ZnO/GQDs are 2.14 MΩ/RH and 0.18 MΩ/RH, respectively. The performed measurements confirmed that the ZnO/GQDs achieved a high response and recovery when compared to bare ZnO.

Dina M. Abdo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mahmoud Rasly: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Mohamed Morsy: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ayat N. El-Shazly: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
No funding has been received for this study.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of coal-based carbon and graphene quantum dots: synthesis, properties, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Mahto, B. Mahanty, S. Hait, S. Hussain, A review of coal-based carbon and graphene quantum dots: synthesis, properties, and applications, Materials Science and Engineering: B 304 (2024) 117386. doi:10.1016/j.mseb.2024.117386.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent studies on the humidity sensor: a Mini review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Arman Kuzubasoglu, Recent Studies on the Humidity Sensor: A Mini Review, ACS Appl Electron Mater 4 (2022) 4797–4807. doi:10.1021/acsaelm.2c00721.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZnO/CuO heterojunction branched nanowires for photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kargar, Y. Jing, S.J. Kim, C.T. Riley, X. Pan, D. Wang, ZnO/CuO heterojunction branched nanowires for photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation, ACS Nano 7 (2013) 11112–11120. doi:10.1021/nn404838n.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of self-bridged ZnO nanowires and their humidity sensing properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Park, D. Lee, B. Kwak, H.S. Lee, S. Lee, B. Yoo, Synthesis of self-bridged ZnO nanowires and their humidity sensing properties, Sens Actuators B Chem 268 (2018) 293–298. doi:10.1016/j.snb.2018.04.118.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced sensing performance of relative humidity sensors using laterally grown ZnO nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.S. Tsai, S.J. Wang, Enhanced sensing performance of relative humidity sensors using laterally grown ZnO nanosheets, Sens Actuators B Chem 193 (2014) 280–287. doi:10.1016/j.snb.2013.11.069.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CuO/ZnO core/shell heterostructure nanowire arrays: synthesis, optical property, and energy application
